symbols: Joseph fleeing — moral courage and resistance to temptation
garment seized by the woman — the false evidence that will be used to accuse the innocent
Potiphar's wife on the bed in red — seductive temptation and the danger of illicit desire
Joseph's turned body — the physical act of turning away from sin
dark curtain — the hidden, private space where temptation operates
checkered floor — the ordered domestic space disrupted by transgression
description: A dramatic Baroque painting showing a young man (Joseph) in mid-flight, pulling away from a seated woman (Potiphar's wife) who grasps his cloak or garment. Joseph wears a tan-brown cloak that the woman grips as he turns to flee, his body in dynamic motion away from her. The woman sits on a bed or couch wearing a rich red dress with exposed shoulders, leaning forward with one hand grasping the garment. The setting is an interior room with a dark curtain, a column, and a checkered floor. The composition captures the exact moment of Joseph's flight with dramatic tension between the two figures.
theological_description: The encounter between Joseph and Potiphar's wife (Genesis 39:6-20) is one of Scripture's most vivid accounts of moral heroism in the face of temptation. Potiphar's wife 'cast her eyes on Joseph and said, Lie with me,' but Joseph refused: 'How then can I do this great wickedness and sin against God?' (Genesis 39:7-9). When she seized his garment, 'he left his garment in her hand and fled and got out of the house' (Genesis 39:12). Joseph's response embodies the scriptural principle Paul later articulated: 'Flee from sexual immorality' (1 Corinthians 6:18) — not to negotiate with temptation but to physically flee. The abandoned garment became false evidence leading to Joseph's imprisonment, yet this apparent injustice was part of God's sovereign plan: 'You meant evil against me, but God meant it for good' (Genesis 50:20). Joseph's resistance prefigures Christ's resistance to Satan's temptations (Matthew 4:1-11) and models the Proverbs' wisdom: 'Can a man carry fire next to his chest and his clothes not be burned?' (Proverbs 6:27). The painting's dramatic tension between Joseph's righteous flight and the woman's grasping seduction visually captures James 4:7: 'Resist the devil, and he will flee from you.'

scripture_refs.reason: Joseph refusing Potiphar's wife and fleeing — the event directly depicted
How can I do this great wickedness and sin against God — Joseph's theological reasoning
Flee from sexual immorality — Joseph as the biblical exemplar of this command
You meant evil but God meant it for good — the providential outcome of Joseph's suffering
Can a man carry fire and not be burned — the wisdom of fleeing rather than lingering
